profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `AllocProfile.jl`) ## Analysis Can use https://github.com/vilterp/AllocProfileParser.jl to rehydrate the files and work with them a bit more easily in Julia. Maybe that library could be used to munge the data into FlameGraphs / PProf. ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [ ] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `AllocProfile.jl`) ## Analysis Can use https://github.com/vilterp/AllocProfileParser.jl to rehydrate the files and work with them a bit more easily in Julia. Maybe that library could be used to munge the data into FlameGraphs / PProf. ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [ ] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `AllocProfile.jl`) ## Analysis Can use https://github.com/vilterp/AllocProfileParser.jl to rehydrate the files and work with them a bit more easily in Julia. Maybe that library could be used to munge the data into FlameGraphs / PProf. ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 This can be enabled with [`GC.enable_logging(true)`](@ref), which causes Julia to log to stderr every time a garbage collection happens. +### Allocation Profiler + +The allocation profiler records the stack trace, type, and size of each +allocation while it is running. It can be invoked with +[`Profile.Allocs.@profile`](@ref). + +This information about the allocations is returned as an array of `Alloc` +objects, wrapped in an `AllocResults` object. The best way to visualize +these is currently with the [PProf.jl](https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl) +library, which can visualize the call stacks which are making the most +allocations. + +The allocation profiler does have significant overhead, so a `sample_rate` +argument can be passed to speed it up by making it skip some allocations. +Passing `sample_rate=1.0` will make it record everything (which is slow); +`sample_rate=0.1` will record only 10% of the allocations (faster), etc. profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 collector"},{"id":3242880567,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjQyODgwNTY3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/profiler","name":"profiler","color":"9A315A","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":19,"created_at":"2021-10-22T20:58:01Z","updated_at":"2022-01-13T17:22:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42768","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); if I understand correctly, there are two types of stack frames: instruction pointers (to either C code or generated Julia code) and interpreter frames (Julia code being interpreted at runtime). The latter frames only show up as "unknown" in CPU and allocation profiles.

This is because both the CPU profiler (module `Profile`) and the allocation profiler in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768 (module `Profile.Allocs`) use `StackTraces.lookup` to decode stack frames raw backtrace buffers, which works when the backtrace entries are pointers to generated code, but not when they're interpreter frames.

Interpreter frames have a more complicated encoding, and point to objects on the heap. The function `reformat_bt` in `errors.jl` can handle decoding, but it doesn't work well in a profiling setting, because the interpreter frame objects pointed to the backtrace entries can be garbage collected while the profiler is still running, before it has a chance to decode them!

In another memory profiling PR, @maleadt solved this problem by modifying the garbage collector's mark phase to find these pointers in backtrace buffers, thereby preventing them from being collected. Perhaps that change can be merged separately, so then both the allocation and CPU profilers can use `reformat_bt`, and include interpreter frames.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43796/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43796/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1012380410","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43796#issuecomment-1012380410","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43796","id":1012380410,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM48V7L6","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-13T18:04:53Z","updated_at":"2022-01-13T18:04:53Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Thanks; profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Co-Authored-By: @NHDaly profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and print to an output stream as JSON. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Co-Authored-By: @NHDaly Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and return as Julia objects. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Co-Authored-By: @NHDaly profiler","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":195227131,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxOTUyMjcxMzE=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/GC","name":"GC","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"Garbage collector"},{"id":3242880567,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjQyODgwNTY3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/profiler","name":"profiler","color":"9A315A","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":31,"created_at":"2021-10-22T20:58:01Z","updated_at":"2022-01-20T13:32:25Z","closed_at":"2022-01-19T20:51:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42768","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.patch","merged_at":"2022-01-19T20:51:01Z"},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and return as Julia objects. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure collector"},{"id":3242880567,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjQyODgwNTY3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/profiler","name":"profiler","color":"9A315A","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":33,"created_at":"2021-10-22T20:58:01Z","updated_at":"2022-01-20T13:51:31Z","closed_at":"2022-01-19T20:51:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42768","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.patch","merged_at":"2022-01-19T20:51:01Z"},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and return as Julia objects. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Co-Authored-By: @NHDaly ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42768/reactions","total_count":7,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":7,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42768/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1017523685","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768#issuecomment-1017523685","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42768","id":1017523685,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM48pi3l","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-20T13:51:31Z","updated_at":"2022-01-20T13:51:31Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"k, the fix has passed `analyzegc`; {"action":"opened","number":43889,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/43889","id":829139258,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM4xa6k6","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43889","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43889.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43889.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43889","number":43889,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"alloc profile: optimize decoding","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"### Problem

`decode()` in the alloc profiler spends ~50% of time in GC. This is because it constructs a large graph of objects representing each alloc and its stack trace, which the GC has to mark.

### Solution

Instead of decoding the profile into an array of `Alloc` structs, each of which contains an array of `StackFrame` objects, decode it into arrays of scalars (which the GC can safely skip) and make it _look_ like an array of `Alloc`s. Hopefully we can pass this through to pprof without materializing as much in memory as well.

### Evaluation

```
On a profile of HeapSnapshotParser with 3M allocs:

# before
julia> @time Profile.Allocs.fetch()
20.752397 seconds (17.38 M allocations: 16.735 GiB, 64.56% gc time)

# after
julia> @time Profile.Allocs.fetch()
  0.838798 seconds (3.27 M allocations: 1.016 GiB, 53.57% gc time)
# note that it'll still take more time to get a pprof protobuf of this
# but at this stage we can already explore with `length(prof)` and `prof[i]`, `prof.alloc_type[i]` etc
```

### TODO

- evaluate end-to-end perf including pprof
- maybe `AllocResults` should still have a member called `allocs` that implements the array interface, so we can add `frees` later

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43889","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43889/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43889/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43889/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43889","id":1111161178,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM4xa6k6","number":43889,"title":"alloc profile: optimize decoding","user":{"login":"vilterp","id":7341,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7341?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp","html_url":"https://github.com/vilterp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vilterp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-01-22T01:16:31Z","updated_at":"2022-01-24T05:29:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/43889","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43889","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43889.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43889.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"### Problem

`Profile.Alloc.decode()` spends ~50% of time in GC. This is because it constructs a large graph of objects representing each alloc and its stack trace, which the GC has to traverse during each mark phase.

### Solution

Instead of decoding the profile into an array of `Alloc` structs, each of which contains an array of `StackFrame` structs, decode it into arrays of scalars (which the GC can safely skip) and make it _look_ like an array of `Alloc`s. Hopefully we can pass this through to pprof without materializing as much in memory as well.

### Evaluation

```julia
# On a profile of HeapSnapshotParser with 3M allocs:

# before
julia> @time Profile.Allocs.fetch()
20.752397 seconds (17.38 M allocations: 16.735 GiB, 64.56% gc time)

# after
julia> @time Profile.Allocs.fetch()
  0.838798 seconds (3.27 M allocations: 1.016 GiB, 53.57% gc time)
# note that it'll still take more time to get a pprof protobuf of this
# but at this stage we can already explore with `length(prof)` and `prof[i]`, `prof.alloc_type[i]` etc
```

### TODO

- evaluate end-to-end perf including pprof
- maybe `AllocResults` should still have a member called `allocs` that implements the array interface, so we can add `frees` later
